questions.text: I instinctively create structure where there is none.
I proactively organize shared spaces and group projects because order enables progress.
I believe that processing human pain as information to be understood, rather than as an emotional experience to be shared, is a legitimate form of caring.
I let open questions simmer in the background and trust answers will surface when ready.
I treat each day as a standalone experience rather than a chapter in a larger arc.
I tend to think of my possessions as shared resources, not private property.
I believe my professional network exists primarily so I can connect people who need each other.
I tend to guard my weekends for personal projects and rest.
I sense when a group is tacitly agreeing to lower the emotional register of a conversation to keep things safe.
I do not naturally read the social meaning behind gift-giving or gesture exchanges.
I hold the view that self-determination means consistently choosing what's best for me.
A smell connected to a past experience can instantly transport me into the full emotional state of that time.
When I'm excited about a future event, the excitement builds to a pitch that makes it hard to concentrate on anything else.
I write detailed instructions when handing off work to others.
When I become aware of suffering, I find it difficult to justify inaction to myself.
I hold the view that my even temperament is a reflection of my biology, not a choice I make.
I rarely pick up on when a person's compliment is backhanded.
I find that I'm rarely caught off guard by the intensity of my own emotional responses.
I detect unspoken competition between people in professional settings.
I believe that the skill of remaining objective during emotionally intense situations is a contribution to the common good that deserves more recognition than it typically receives.
I prefer that post-mortems be a shared reflection rather than one person's assessment.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the inner signal that most reliably tells me whether I am living in alignment with my stated values.
I think that approaching others' suffering with a clear, rational framework allows me to allocate attention and effort where they will matter most.
I routinely opt for premium personal experiences—travel, dining, learning—because I believe I am worth it.
I carry tasks in my head and address them as they come to mind.
I tend to take what people say at face value.
I instinctively delegate tasks and coordinate efforts.
I believe that my emotional equilibrium is something I was born with rather than something I cultivated.
I find that the weight I give to suffering in my decisions is what makes those decisions feel morally complete.
I experience boredom not as a quiet absence but as an agitating restlessness.
I feel that my primary function in relationships is to give, not to receive.
I am most productive when direction has been established collectively.
I archive completed work in labeled folders so I can find it years from now.
I am the first to speak up when a group needs someone to take ownership of a problem.
I notice when a compliment is designed to establish dominance rather than express admiration.
I often position myself as a facilitator in group settings.
